devanagari: अथ चतुर्थोऽध्यायः । श्रीशुक उवाच । तदा देवर्षिगन्धर्वा ब्रह्मेशानपुरोगमाः । मुमुचुः कुसुमासारं शंसन्तः कर्म तद्धरेः ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: atha caturtho śrīśuka uvāca tadā devarṣigandharvā brahmeśānapurogamāḥ mumucuḥ kusumāsāraṃ śaṃsantaḥ karma taddhareḥ

translation:
Śrī Śuka continued:
Then (seeing the rescue of the lord of elephants), gods headed by Brahmā and Śiva, sages and Gandharvas extolled that mighty deed of Hari, and discharged a shower of flowers on him.

devanagari: नेदुर्दुन्दुभयो दिव्या गन्धर्वा ननृतुर्जगुः । ऋषयश्चारणाः सिद्धास्तुष्टुवुः पुरुषोत्तमम् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: nedurdundubhayo divyā gandharvā nanṛturjaguḥ ṛṣayaścāraṇāḥ siddhāstuṣṭuvuḥ puruṣottamam

translation: Celestial kettle-drums were sounded, the Gandharvas danced and sang; sages, Cāraṇas and Siddhas eulogised the Supreme Person.

devanagari: योऽसौ ग्राहः स वै सद्यः परमाश्चर्यरूपधृक् । मुक्तो देवलशापेन हूहूर्गन्धर्वसत्तमः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: yo mukto devalaśāpena hūhūrgandharvasattamaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: Verily, he who was (till then) the alligator, instantly assumed the most wonderful form. He was Hūhū, the foremost among the Gandharvas, who was thus redeemed from the curse of the Sage Devala.[1]
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā narrates the cause of the curse as follows:—Once while Hūhū was indulging in watersport with his women in a lake, the sage Devala entered the same lake for bath. Hūhū was upset and he pulled Devala by his leg. The sage cursed him to be an alligator. When entreated, Devala advised him to seize the lord of elephants that way, so that Lord Hari will emancipate him and he would be reinstated to his original position automatically.

devanagari: प्रणम्य शिरसाधीशमुत्तमश्लोकमव्ययम् । अगायत यशोधाम कीर्तन्यगुणसत्कथम् ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: praṇamya śirasādhīśamuttamaślokamavyayam agāyata yaśodhāma kīrtanyaguṇasatkatham
translation: He bowed down to the eternal Supreme Lord of excellent renown with his head, and he chanted the praise of his worth extolling (innumerable) virtues and sacred episodes of that abode of glory.

devanagari: सोऽनुकम्पित ईशेन परिक्रम्य प्रणम्य तम् । लोकस्य पश्यतो लोकं स्वमगान्मुक्तकिल्बिषः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: so lokasya paśyato lokaṃ svamagānmuktakilbiṣaḥ
translation: Being favoured by the Lord with his grace, he was purged of all sins. Going round the Lord (reverentially) and paying him obeisance, he repaired to his own region (of Gandharvas) in the very presence of all the people.

devanagari: गजेन्भगवत्स्पर्शाद्विमुक्तोऽज्ञानबन्धनात् । प्राप्तो भगवतो रूपं पीतवासाश्चतुर्भुजः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: gajenbhagavatsparśādvimukto prāpto bhagavato rūpaṃ pītavāsāścaturbhujaḥ
translation: By virtue of his being touched by the Lord, the lord of elephants became completely free from the bondage of ignorance, and attained to the form resembling that of the Lord (Viṣṇu) clad in yellow garments and endowed with four arms.

devanagari: स वै पूर्वमभूद्रा जा पाण्ड्यो द्र विडसत्तमः । इन्द्र द्युम्न इति ख्यातो विष्णुव्रतपरायणः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: sa vai pūrvamabhūdrā jā pāṇḍyo dra viḍasattamaḥ indra dyumna iti khyāto viṣṇuvrataparāyaṇaḥ

translation: It is traditionally reported that he was in his previous birth a king of the Pāṇḍya country well-known as Indradyumna—the foremost among the Draviḍa people—and devotedly attached to the austere observance of Viṣṇu-worship.

devanagari: स एकदाराधनकाल आत्मवान्गृहीतमौनव्रत ईश्वरं हरिम् । जटाधरस्तापस आप्लुतोऽच्युतं समर्चयामास कुलाचलाश्रमः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: sa ekadārādhanakāla ātmavāngṛhītamaunavrata īśvaraṃ harim jaṭādharastāpasa āpluto
translation: Once upon a time, the king who, as an ascetic, wearing matted locks of hair, was staying in his hermitage on the Malaya mountain, had undertaken the vow of silence. At the appointed hour of the propitiation of the Lord, he took his bath and began to worship with due formalities, the imperishable supreme Ruler, Lord Hari.

devanagari: यदृच्छया तत्र महायशा मुनिः समागमच्छिष्यगणैः परिश्रितः । तं वीक्ष्य तूष्णीमकृतार्हणादिकं रहस्युपासीनमृषिश्चुकोप ह ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: yadṛcchayā tatra mahāyaśā muniḥ samāgamacchiṣyagaṇaiḥ pariśritaḥ taṃ vīkṣya tūṣṇīmakṛtārhaṇādikaṃ rahasyupāsīnamṛṣiścukopa ha

translation: (It is reported that) by chance there arrived (at his hermitage) the celebrated sage Agastya accompanied by hosts of his disciples. Finding Indradyumna seated in solitude and remaining silent (without showing the courtesy of greeting orally) and without paying the due homage (usual formalities of receiving a guest of honour) the sage flared up in rage.

devanagari: तस्मा इमं शापमदादसाधुरयं दुरात्माकृतबुद्धिरद्य । विप्रावमन्ता विशतां तमिस्रं यथा गजः स्तब्धमतिः स एव ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: tasmā imaṃ śāpamadādasādhurayaṃ durātmākṛtabuddhiradya viprāvamantā viśatāṃ tamisraṃ yathā gajaḥ stabdhamatiḥ sa eva

translation: He pronounced the following imprecation on the king: “This sinful, uncultured, wicked soul has today insulted a Brāhmaṇa (like me). He is dull-witted like an elephant. Let him be born in the species of elephants steeped in blinding darkness of ignorance.”

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । एवं शप्त्वा गतोऽगस्त्यो भगवान्नृप सानुगः । इन्द्र द्युम्नोऽपि राजर्षिर्दिष्टं तदुपधारयन् ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvāca evaṃ śaptvā gato indra dyumno

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Oh king! Having uttered this curse, the venerable Agastya went way along with his followers. The royal sage Indradyumna, on his part, considered the incident to be the will of the Providence.

devanagari: आपन्नः कौञ्जरीं योनिमात्मस्मृतिविनाशिनीम् । हर्यर्चनानुभावेन यद्गजत्वेऽप्यनुस्मृतिः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: āpannaḥ kauñjarīṃ yonimātmasmṛtivināśinīm haryarcanānubhāvena yadgajatve

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: He entered the species of elephants which obscures the knowledge of the self. But by virtue of his propitiation of Hari the reminiscence of his past life remained unblurred (even while he was an elephant).

devanagari: एवं विमोक्ष्य गजयूथपमब्जनाभस् । तेनापि पार्षदगतिं गमितेन युक्तः । गन्धर्वसिद्धविबुधैरुपगीयमान । कर्माद्भुतं स्वभवनं गरुडासनोऽगात् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ vimokṣya gajayūthapamabjanābhas tenāpi pārṣadagatiṃ gamitena yuktaḥ gandharvasiddhavibudhairupagīyamāna karmādbhutaṃ svabhavanaṃ garuḍāsano

translation: Having thus rescued the lord of the herd of elephants and accompanied by him after elevating him to the position of his attendant, the lotus-navelled god (Viṣṇu) seated on Garuḍa repaired to his region Vaikuṇṭha, while Gandharvas, Siddhas and gods were glorifying his miraculous feat.

devanagari: एतन्महाराज तवेरितो मया कृष्णानुभावो गजराजमोक्षणम् । स्वर्ग्यं यशस्यं कलिकल्मषापहं दुःस्वप्ननाशं कुरुवर्य शृण्वताम् ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: etanmahārāja taverito mayā kṛṣṇānubhāvo gajarājamokṣaṇam svargyaṃ yaśasyaṃ kalikalmaṣāpahaṃ duḥsvapnanāśaṃ kuruvarya śṛṇvatām
translation: This glorious deed of Kṛṣṇa (Viṣṇu), viz. The Liberation of King of elephants, has been narrated to you by me, Oh great king. Listening to this leads to heaven, confers glory, removes the sins of the Kali age and destroys the effect of bad dreams of the hearers, Oh great Kuru.

devanagari: यथानुकीर्तयन्त्येतच्छ्रेयस्कामा द्विजातयः । शुचयः प्रातरुत्थाय दुःस्वप्नाद्युपशान्तये ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: yathānukīrtayantyetacchreyaskāmā dvijātayaḥ śucayaḥ prātarutthāya duḥsvapnādyupaśāntaye

translation: Persons belonging to the twice-born castes desiring to secure their welfare, get up early in the morning, and purifying themselves (after taking a bath) recite this sacred narration (of the liberation of the elephant king) for obliterating the effects of bad dreams (and securing their welfare).

devanagari: इदमाह हरिः प्रीतो गजेन्द्रं कुरुसत्तम । शृण्वतां सर्वभूतानां सर्वभूतमयो विभुः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: idamāha hariḥ prīto gajendraṃ kurusattama śṛṇvatāṃ sarvabhūtānāṃ sarvabhūtamayo vibhuḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ prī class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ prī class 9 verb]

translation: It was while all creatures were listening that the Omnipresent Lord Hari who embodies all creation, being highly pleased with the Lord of elephants, addressed the following (words) to him; Oh the best of Kurus.

devanagari: श्रीभगवानुवाच । ये मां त्वां च सरश्चेदं गिरिकन्दरकाननम् । वेत्रकीचकवेणूनां गुल्मानि सुरपादपान् ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: śrībhagavānuvāca ye māṃ tvāṃ ca saraścedaṃ girikandarakānanam vetrakīcakaveṇūnāṃ gulmāni surapādapān

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: Rising up at the close of the latter part of the night i.e. at dawn (known as Brāhma muhūrta), and after purifying themselves (by taking a bath), those who, with concentrated mind, remember me, you, this lake, the mountain (Trikūṭa) along with its caves and valleys and the forest (covering them), the clusters of canes and Kīcaka bamboos, the heavenly trees, the (three) mountain peaks, the abodes of Brahmā, Śiva and myself, the ocean of milk, my favourite resort, the resplendent Śveta-dvīpa (My beloved place), the Śrīvatsa mark (the characteristic curl of hair) on my breast, the Kaustubha gem, and the garland known as Vaijayantī, my weapons, the mace called Kaumodakī, the discus Sudarśana, the conch Pāñcajanya, my vehicle Garuḍa, the king of birds, Lord Saṅkarṣaṇa who is my subtle emanation, goddess Śrī (Lakṣmī) who rests on my bosom, god Brahmā, the divine sage Nārada, god Śiva and (my devotee) Prahlāda, and all my deeds performed during my incarnations as Fish, Tortoise, Boar and others—listening to which confers infinite merit (to the hearers), the Sun-god, the Moon-god, the Fire-god, the sacred syllable OM, the Eternal Truth, the unmanifest (Prakṛti), Cows and Brāhmaṇas, the eternal path of religion (consisting of devotion unto the Lord, the daughters of Dakṣa who are the wives of Dharma, Soma and Kaśyapa, the rivers—the Gaṅgā, the Sarasvatī, the Nandā, the Yamunā, the White elephant of Indra (Airāvata), Dhruva, the Seven Brāhmaṇa Sages and great men of hallowed fame, (such as Nala, Yudhiṣṭhira, Janaka, the king of Videha)—all these being my forms, those who remember them, become free from all sins.

devanagari: शृङ्गाणीमानि धिष्ण्यानि ब्रह्मणो मे शिवस्य च । क्षीरोदं मे प्रियं धाम श्वेतद्वीपं च भास्वरम् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: śṛṅgāṇīmāni dhiṣṇyāni brahmaṇo me śivasya ca kṣīrodaṃ me priyaṃ dhāma śvetadvīpaṃ ca bhāsvaram
translation: Rising up at the close of the latter part of the night i.e. at dawn (known as Brāhma muhūrta), and after purifying themselves (by taking a bath), those who, with concentrated mind, remember me, you, this lake, the mountain (Trikūṭa) along with its caves and valleys and the forest (covering them), the clusters of canes and Kīcaka bamboos, the heavenly trees, the (three) mountain peaks, the abodes of Brahmā, Śiva and myself, the ocean of milk, my favourite resort, the resplendent Śveta-dvīpa (My beloved place), the Śrīvatsa mark (the characteristic curl of hair) on my breast, the Kaustubha gem, and the garland known as Vaijayantī, my weapons, the mace called Kaumodakī, the discus Sudarśana, the conch Pāñcajanya, my vehicle Garuḍa, the king of birds, Lord Saṅkarṣaṇa who is my subtle emanation, goddess Śrī (Lakṣmī) who rests on my bosom, god Brahmā, the divine sage Nārada, god Śiva and (my devotee) Prahlāda, and all my deeds performed during my incarnations as Fish, Tortoise, Boar and others—listening to which confers infinite merit (to the hearers), the Sun-god, the Moon-god, the Fire-god, the sacred syllable OM, the Eternal Truth, the unmanifest (Prakṛti), Cows and Brāhmaṇas, the eternal path of religion (consisting of devotion unto the Lord, the daughters of Dakṣa who are the wives of Dharma, Soma and Kaśyapa, the rivers—the Gaṅgā, the Sarasvatī, the Nandā, the Yamunā, the White elephant of Indra (Airāvata), Dhruva, the Seven Brāhmaṇa Sages and great men of hallowed fame, (such as Nala, Yudhiṣṭhira, Janaka, the king of Videha)—all these being my forms, those who remember them, become free from all sins.

devanagari: श्रीवत्सं कौस्तुभं मालां गदां कौमोदकीं मम । सुदर्शनं पाञ्चजन्यं सुपर्णं पतगेश्वरम् ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīvatsaṃ kaustubhaṃ mālāṃ gadāṃ kaumodakīṃ mama sudarśanaṃ pāñcajanyaṃ suparṇaṃ patageśvaram
translation: Rising up at the close of the latter part of the night i.e. at dawn (known as Brāhma muhūrta), and after purifying themselves (by taking a bath), those who, with concentrated mind, remember me, you, this lake, the mountain (Trikūṭa) along with its caves and valleys and the forest (covering them), the clusters of canes and Kīcaka bamboos, the heavenly trees, the (three) mountain peaks, the abodes of Brahmā, Śiva and myself, the ocean of milk, my favourite resort, the resplendent Śveta-dvīpa (My beloved place), the Śrīvatsa mark (the characteristic curl of hair) on my breast, the Kaustubha gem, and the garland known as Vaijayantī, my weapons, the mace called Kaumodakī, the discus Sudarśana, the conch Pāñcajanya, my vehicle Garuḍa, the king of birds, Lord Saṅkarṣaṇa who is my subtle emanation, goddess Śrī (Lakṣmī) who rests on my bosom, god Brahmā, the divine sage Nārada, god Śiva and (my devotee) Prahlāda, and all my deeds performed during my incarnations as Fish, Tortoise, Boar and others—listening to which confers infinite merit (to the hearers), the Sun-god, the Moon-god, the Fire-god, the sacred syllable OM, the Eternal Truth, the unmanifest (Prakṛti), Cows and Brāhmaṇas, the eternal path of religion (consisting of devotion unto the Lord, the daughters of Dakṣa who are the wives of Dharma, Soma and Kaśyapa, the rivers—the Gaṅgā, the Sarasvatī, the Nandā, the Yamunā, the White elephant of Indra (Airāvata), Dhruva, the Seven Brāhmaṇa Sages and great men of hallowed fame, (such as Nala, Yudhiṣṭhira, Janaka, the king of Videha)—all these being my forms, those who remember them, become free from all sins.

devanagari: शेषं च मत्कलां सूक्ष्मां श्रियं देवीं मदाश्रयाम् । ब्रह्माणं नारदमृषिं भवं प्रह्रादमेव च ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: śeṣaṃ ca matkalāṃ sūkṣmāṃ śriyaṃ devīṃ madāśrayām brahmāṇaṃ nāradamṛṣiṃ bhavaṃ prahrādameva ca
translation: Rising up at the close of the latter part of the night i.e. at dawn (known as Brāhma muhūrta), and after purifying themselves (by taking a bath), those who, with concentrated mind, remember me, you, this lake, the mountain (Trikūṭa) along with its caves and valleys and the forest (covering them), the clusters of canes and Kīcaka bamboos, the heavenly trees, the (three) mountain peaks, the abodes of Brahmā, Śiva and myself, the ocean of milk, my favourite resort, the resplendent Śveta-dvīpa (My beloved place), the Śrīvatsa mark (the characteristic curl of hair) on my breast, the Kaustubha gem, and the garland known as Vaijayantī, my weapons, the mace called Kaumodakī, the discus Sudarśana, the conch Pāñcajanya, my vehicle Garuḍa, the king of birds, Lord Saṅkarṣaṇa who is my subtle emanation, goddess Śrī (Lakṣmī) who rests on my bosom, god Brahmā, the divine sage Nārada, god Śiva and (my devotee) Prahlāda, and all my deeds performed during my incarnations as Fish, Tortoise, Boar and others—listening to which confers infinite merit (to the hearers), the Sun-god, the Moon-god, the Fire-god, the sacred syllable OM, the Eternal Truth, the unmanifest (Prakṛti), Cows and Brāhmaṇas, the eternal path of religion (consisting of devotion unto the Lord, the daughters of Dakṣa who are the wives of Dharma, Soma and Kaśyapa, the rivers—the Gaṅgā, the Sarasvatī, the Nandā, the Yamunā, the White elephant of Indra (Airāvata), Dhruva, the Seven Brāhmaṇa Sages and great men of hallowed fame, (such as Nala, Yudhiṣṭhira, Janaka, the king of Videha)—all these being my forms, those who remember them, become free from all sins.

devanagari: मत्स्यकूर्मवराहाद्यैरवतारैः कृतानि मे । कर्माण्यनन्तपुण्यानि सूर्यं सोमं हुताशनम् ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: matsyakūrmavarāhādyairavatāraiḥ kṛtāni me karmāṇyanantapuṇyāni sūryaṃ somaṃ hutāśanam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

[nominative single from √ sūr class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ sūr class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ sūr class 10 verb], [accusative single from √ sūr class 10 verb]

translation: Rising up at the close of the latter part of the night i.e. at dawn (known as Brāhma muhūrta), and after purifying themselves (by taking a bath), those who, with concentrated mind, remember me, you, this lake, the mountain (Trikūṭa) along with its caves and valleys and the forest (covering them), the clusters of canes and Kīcaka bamboos, the heavenly trees, the (three) mountain peaks, the abodes of Brahmā, Śiva and myself, the ocean of milk, my favourite resort, the resplendent Śveta-dvīpa (My beloved place), the Śrīvatsa mark (the characteristic curl of hair) on my breast, the Kaustubha gem, and the garland known as Vaijayantī, my weapons, the mace called Kaumodakī, the discus Sudarśana, the conch Pāñcajanya, my vehicle Garuḍa, the king of birds, Lord Saṅkarṣaṇa who is my subtle emanation, goddess Śrī (Lakṣmī) who rests on my bosom, god Brahmā, the divine sage Nārada, god Śiva and (my devotee) Prahlāda, and all my deeds performed during my incarnations as Fish, Tortoise, Boar and others—listening to which confers infinite merit (to the hearers), the Sun-god, the Moon-god, the Fire-god, the sacred syllable OM, the Eternal Truth, the unmanifest (Prakṛti), Cows and Brāhmaṇas, the eternal path of religion (consisting of devotion unto the Lord, the daughters of Dakṣa who are the wives of Dharma, Soma and Kaśyapa, the rivers—the Gaṅgā, the Sarasvatī, the Nandā, the Yamunā, the White elephant of Indra (Airāvata), Dhruva, the Seven Brāhmaṇa Sages and great men of hallowed fame, (such as Nala, Yudhiṣṭhira, Janaka, the king of Videha)—all these being my forms, those who remember them, become free from all sins.

devanagari: प्रणवं सत्यमव्यक्तं गोविप्रान्धर्ममव्ययम् । दाक्षायणीर्धर्मपत्नीः सोमकश्यपयोरपि ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: praṇavaṃ satyamavyaktaṃ goviprāndharmamavyayam dākṣāyaṇīrdharmapatnīḥ somakaśyapayorapi
translation: Rising up at the close of the latter part of the night i.e. at dawn (known as Brāhma muhūrta), and after purifying themselves (by taking a bath), those who, with concentrated mind, remember me, you, this lake, the mountain (Trikūṭa) along with its caves and valleys and the forest (covering them), the clusters of canes and Kīcaka bamboos, the heavenly trees, the (three) mountain peaks, the abodes of Brahmā, Śiva and myself, the ocean of milk, my favourite resort, the resplendent Śveta-dvīpa (My beloved place), the Śrīvatsa mark (the characteristic curl of hair) on my breast, the Kaustubha gem, and the garland known as Vaijayantī, my weapons, the mace called Kaumodakī, the discus Sudarśana, the conch Pāñcajanya, my vehicle Garuḍa, the king of birds, Lord Saṅkarṣaṇa who is my subtle emanation, goddess Śrī (Lakṣmī) who rests on my bosom, god Brahmā, the divine sage Nārada, god Śiva and (my devotee) Prahlāda, and all my deeds performed during my incarnations as Fish, Tortoise, Boar and others—listening to which confers infinite merit (to the hearers), the Sun-god, the Moon-god, the Fire-god, the sacred syllable OM, the Eternal Truth, the unmanifest (Prakṛti), Cows and Brāhmaṇas, the eternal path of religion (consisting of devotion unto the Lord, the daughters of Dakṣa who are the wives of Dharma, Soma and Kaśyapa, the rivers—the Gaṅgā, the Sarasvatī, the Nandā, the Yamunā, the White elephant of Indra (Airāvata), Dhruva, the Seven Brāhmaṇa Sages and great men of hallowed fame, (such as Nala, Yudhiṣṭhira, Janaka, the king of Videha)—all these being my forms, those who remember them, become free from all sins.

devanagari: गङ्गां सरस्वतीं नन्दां कालिन्दीं सितवारणम् । ध्रुवं ब्रह्मऋषीन्सप्त पुण्यश्लोकांश्च मानवान् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: gaṅgāṃ sarasvatīṃ nandāṃ kālindīṃ sitavāraṇam dhruvaṃ brahmaṛṣīnsapta puṇyaślokāṃśca mānavān

devanagari: उत्थायापररात्रान्ते प्रयताः सुसमाहिताः । स्मरन्ति मम रूपाणि मुच्यन्ते ह्येनसोऽखिलात् ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: utthāyāpararātrānte prayatāḥ susamāhitāḥ smaranti mama rūpāṇi mucyante hyenaso

devanagari: ये मां स्तुवन्त्यनेनाङ्ग प्रतिबुध्य निशात्यये । तेषां प्राणात्यये चाहं ददामि विपुलां गतिम् ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: ye māṃ stuvantyanenāṅga pratibudhya niśātyaye teṣāṃ prāṇātyaye cāhaṃ dadāmi vipulāṃ gatim

translation: Oh dear Gajendra! To those who, after getting up at dawn (after the expiry of night), pray to me with the hymn (composed by you), I promise to keep their memory unclouded at the time of their death.

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । इत्यादिश्य हृषीकेशः प्राध्माय जलजोत्तमम् । हर्षयन्विबुधानीकमारुरोह खगाधिपम् ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvāca ityādiśya hṛṣīkeśaḥ prādhmāya jalajottamam harṣayanvibudhānīkamāruroha khagādhipam

[nominative single from √ hṛṣ ], [vocative single from √ hṛṣ ]

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Having addressed thus, Lord Viṣṇu (the Lord of senses) blew his excellent conch (Pāñcajanya) gladdening thereby the host of gods and mounted his carrier Garuḍa, the king of birds.
